danger_signals.visual_form: Distinct colored enemy sprites (purple, grey, flame-themed variants)
danger_signals.meaning: Hostile forces that deal damage; detection triggers security response

danger_signals.visual_form: Flame-thrower wall traps, bombs, debris from destructible objects
danger_signals.meaning: Stationary or triggered damage sources that reduce player health

danger_signals.visual_form: Not visually detailed in sources, but mechanically present
danger_signals.meaning: Alerting guards when player is spotted during infiltration

danger_signals.visual_form: NPCs in open areas during initial stages
danger_signals.meaning: Harming civilians triggers arrest and mission failure

safety_signals.meaning: Areas where stealth and combat are primary concerns rather than civilian restrictions

safety_signals.visual_form: UI affordance allowing player to switch between Max, Grant, Nicholas
safety_signals.meaning: Access to different skill sets for solving environmental puzzles and challenges

faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Player-controlled operatives represent IMF; no explicit visual branding documented

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Multiple enemy types with distinct colors (purple Pistol Pete Piranovich, grey Slash Stiletto, flame-thrower variants Fido T. Flamethrower and Jorgé Burns, stationary knights)
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Enemy hideouts across Venice, Swiss Alps, Moscow, Berlin, Cyprus locations
Present in street-level areas; arrest player if civilians harmed

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Drab color palette with civilian NPCs present; non-hostile initial setting
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player restricted from combat; must avoid harming civilians; detection by authorities results in arrest and mission failure

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Multi-floor interior installations with distinct enemy placement; surveillance systems implied
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Combat and stealth are primary mechanics; detection triggers guard response; discretion advised

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Enemy sprites distributed throughout levels; environmental traps visible (flame walls, bombs, destructible objects)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Enemies deal damage based on regional difficulty (US version: higher damage; EU version: reduced damage)

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Environmental elements requiring specific character skills (manhole covers, locked doors, obstacles)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player switches between Max, Grant, Nicholas to leverage different abilities

environmental_storytelling.example: Mission takes place across Venice canals, Swiss Alps, Moscow, Berlin, Cyprus—geographic scope communicates escalating stakes and international scope of terrorist threat
Different enemy variants (Pistol Pete Piranovich, Fido T. Flamethrower, Jorgé Burns, Slash Stiletto) suggest diverse hostile forces rather than generic soldiers
Vertically-structured hideouts communicate fortress-like security and infiltration challenge complexity
Street-level civilians in opening area establish IMF operatives as undercover agents who must blend in, setting espionage tone

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Throws Molotov cocktails; deals high damage (6 pts US / 2 pts EU per hit)
Uses ranged attacks; deals 2 pts damage (US) / 1 pt (EU) per bullet
Uses flamethrower; final stage enemy with high damage output
Deploys bombs at stage end; grey version indicates late-game escalation
Deal melee damage (2-3 pts US / 1 pt EU); standing variants paired with mobile variants
Stationary damage source; deals 6 pts (US) / 2 pts (EU); triggers on proximity
Respond when surveillance system detects player infiltration
Arrest player if civilians harmed; represents mission failure condition

notes: Mission: Impossible NES communicates world-state primarily through enemy type variation and environmental hazard placement rather than explicit visual symbolism. The paradigm shift from street-level (civilian presence, authority threat) to infiltration (enemy combat, stealth) is behavioral rather than purely visual. Regional difficulty balancing (US version 2-3x damage scaling) suggests different threat perception across markets. Character switching mechanic reinforces puzzle-solving narrative (different operatives for different challenges). Limited color palette ('drab colors' per reviews) constrains faction visual differentiation, relying instead on enemy sprite design and behavior to communicate threat level.
